professor.name: Dr. Halvorsen
professor.text: We've been studying how cities fund public transportation this semester. Several mid-sized cities are now debating whether to eliminate paid street parking downtown and redirect that revenue into cheaper bus and train fares. Some economists argue this would ease congestion and help lower-income commuters, while others warn that losing parking revenue could strain city budgets and hurt local shops that depend on drivers. Do you think cities should make this trade-off? Why or why not?

students.name: Marco
students.text: I think redirecting the money makes sense. Reliable, affordable transit serves far more people than a handful of parking spaces ever could, and cheaper fares disproportionately help those who cannot afford cars. Cities should prioritize the majority of commuters over the convenience of a few drivers.

students.name: Leah
students.text: I'm not convinced the trade-off is worth it. Small downtown businesses rely heavily on customers who drive in, and removing parking could push them toward closing. Without a guaranteed replacement for that revenue, a city risks funding transit while quietly eroding its own commercial core.

professor.name: Dr. Okonkwo
professor.text: We've been examining how newsrooms adopt new tools this semester. A growing number of outlets now use artificial intelligence to draft routine stories such as sports recaps and corporate earnings summaries. Some editors argue that readers deserve a clear label whenever software helped write an article, while others believe such labels are unnecessary and might make audiences distrust perfectly accurate reporting. Should news organizations be required to disclose AI assistance? Why or why not?

students.name: Aisha
students.text: Disclosure should absolutely be required. Readers form judgments about credibility based partly on who—or what—produced a story, and concealing that a machine drafted it removes their ability to weigh the source properly. Transparency costs the outlet almost nothing and meaningfully protects the public's trust.

students.name: Cameron
students.text: I think mandatory labels do more harm than good. If an article is accurate and edited by professionals, the tool used to draft it is largely irrelevant to its quality. Attaching a warning mainly feeds an unfounded suspicion of technology rather than informing anyone.

professor.name: Dr. Reyes
professor.text: For this week's discussion, let's think about how large introductory courses are taught. Many universities still pack hundreds of first-year students into a single lecture hall, but a few are now breaking those courses into small discussion sections led by graduate instructors. Some faculty argue smaller groups deepen understanding and keep students engaged, while others contend that lectures deliver expert knowledge more efficiently and consistently. Which approach do you think serves beginning students better?

students.name: Yuki
students.text: Small discussion sections are clearly better for beginners. Students learn far more when they can ask questions, defend ideas, and receive feedback than when they passively absorb a lecture. Early engagement also builds the habits of participation that deeper coursework will later demand of them.

students.name: Ross
students.text: I'd defend the traditional lecture for introductory material. A single expert can present a coherent, carefully structured overview that dozens of inexperienced graduate instructors simply cannot match. For foundational facts and frameworks, consistency and depth of expertise matter more than constant discussion.

professor.name: Dr. Bianchi
professor.text: More and more companies now offer employees a discount on their health insurance if they meet certain fitness targets, such as a daily step count or a healthy weight range. Supporters argue these incentives encourage healthier habits and reduce medical costs, while critics warn the programs unfairly penalize people with conditions beyond their control and pressure workers to hand over private health data. Should employers be allowed to tie insurance premiums to fitness goals? Why or why not?

students.name: Diane
students.text: I support these incentives. Chronic illness drives enormous costs that everyone ultimately shares through higher premiums, so rewarding healthier behavior seems both fair and practical. As long as the targets are reasonable and genuinely voluntary, nudging people toward better habits benefits the entire insured population.

students.name: Jared
students.text: This strikes me as quietly coercive. Many factors shaping someone's weight or activity—genetics, disability, demanding schedules—lie well outside personal control, so penalizing those who fall short punishes circumstance, not choice. Private health data should also never influence what a worker pays for basic coverage.
